items.q: Why is the urban heat island effect strongest at night?
items.correct: Concrete and brick released their stored daytime heat slowly into the night air, while rural areas cool rapidly through radiative loss — widening the urban-rural temperature gap after sunset.
items.misconception: Urban heat islands are strongest at midday because cities are sunniest then.
items.random_wrong: Urban heat islands only happen in summer.
items.why: Thermal mass of urban materials is the key — they buffer city temperature. At night, this buffering creates the largest urban-rural difference, sometimes 5-10 degrees C in major cities.

items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: How do glaciers form U-shaped valleys?
items.correct: Glaciers erode existing V-shaped river valleys through plucking (freezing onto rock and pulling pieces away) and abrasion (rock embedded in the ice scraping the valley floor and sides).
items.misconception: Glaciers carve U-shaped valleys from flat land by pushing through.
items.random_wrong: U-shaped valleys form when rivers freeze and expand.
items.why: Glaciers usually deepen pre-existing river valleys, not create them from nothing. Students miss that glacial landscapes inherit and modify earlier fluvial topography.

items.q: Approximately what is the maximum annual precipitation typical of a hot desert (mm)?
items.why: Correct answer: 250. “1,000” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A normally dry channel that floods after rain
items.why: Correct answer: A normally dry channel that floods after rain. “A type of cactus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate area of the Sahara desert (million km²)?
items.why: Exact answer: 9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
